title: Directivity Enhancement of Movable Antenna Arrays with Mutual Coupling
authors: Wei Xu, Lipeng Zhu, Wenyan Ma, An Liu, Rui Zhang
abstract: In conventional antenna arrays, mutual coupling between antenna elements is often regarded as detrimental. However, under specific conditions, it can be harnessed to enhance the far-field directivity (i.e., beamforming gain). Theoretically, the directivity of an N-antenna superdirective array over the endfire direction can reach N2, significantly exceeding the directivity of a traditional uncoupled array which is N over all directions. This paper investigates the potential of mutual coupling effects in movable antenna (MA) arrays for directivity enhancement. A low-complexity algorithm called Greedy Search and Gradient Descent (GS-GD) is proposed to optimize the antenna positions for maximizing the array directivity over any given direction, where the antenna positions are first selected sequentially from discrete grid points and then continuously refined through gradient descent (GD) optimization. Numerical results demonstrate that the optimized MA array design by exploiting the antenna coupling achieves significant directivity gains compared to the conventional uniform linear array (ULA) without antenna coupling over all directions. Additionally, the proposed GS-GD algorithm is shown to approach the global optimum closely in most directions.

title: The AI Research Assistant: Promise, Peril, and a Proof of Concept_supp1-3683758.pdf
authors: Tan Bui-Thanh
abstract:
Can artificial intelligence truly contribute to creative mathematical research, or does it merely automate routine calculations while introducing risks of error? We provide empirical evidence through a detailed case study: the discovery of novel error representations and bounds for Hermite quadrature rules via systematic human-AI collaboration.

Working with multiple AI assistants, we extended results beyond what manual work achieved, formulating and proving several theorems with AI assistance. The collaboration revealed both remarkable capabilities and critical limitations. AI excelled at algebraic manipulation, systematic proof exploration, literature synthesis, and LaTeX preparation. However, every step required rigorous human verification, mathematical intuition for problem formulation, and strategic direction. We document the complete research workflow with unusual transparency, revealing patterns in successful human-AI mathematical collaboration and identifying failure modes researchers must anticipate. Our experience suggests that, when used with appropriate skepticism and verification protocols, AI tools can meaningfully accelerate mathematical discovery while demanding careful human oversight and deep domain expertise.

title: Energy-Efficient Air-Ground Cooperative Detection for UAV-Enabled ISAC Systems
authors: Pengfei Du, Siyu Zhao, Haotong Cao, Ziye Jia, Lei Liu, Jianbo Du, Celimuge Wu
abstract: With the exponential proliferation of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), the “black-fly” UAVs pose a severe threat to the public safety. Fortunately, the UAV-enabled integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system exploits the high mobility, line-of-sight (LoS) dominant links, and multi-antenna arrays to construct omnidirectional sensing fields and reliable communication links, enabling multi-view joint detection and tracking of black-fly UAVs, thus guaranteeing the low-altitude safety. However, the existing UAV-enabled ISAC systems suffer from limited beamforming gain due to small uniform linear arrays (ULAs), degraded extended Kalman filter (EKF) performance under strong nonlinear noise and time-varying multipath, and energy inefficiency stemming from sensing-communication resource competition. To deal with these challenges, we investigate the ground-air cooperative detection problem for UAV-enabled ISAC systems, where a ground base station (BS) cooperates with multiple cellular networks connected UAVs (CUAVs) to detect the black-fly UAVs. Specifically, an EKF-Neural Network (NN) based cooperative sensing data fusion algorithm to fuse the sensing results obtained from the BS and the UAVs for realizling high accurate, long-range, and continuous sensing of back-fly UAVs. Based on the fusion sensing results, we propose an energy-efficient joint beamforming and trajectory optimization algorithm to minimize the total ISAC energy consumption of the CUAVs under the stringent communication rate and sensing beampattern gain constraints, which has a positive feedback to the sensing data fusion. Abundant numerical results demonstrate the proposed strategy obtains the 18.4%–24.1% energy savings, 9.43% higher average communication rate and 27.3% improved sensing beam pattern gain compared with the benchmark schemes.

title: Stress Field Engineering for Ultra-Low Birefringence in Trench-Assisted Multi-Core Fibers
authors: Gustavo Ocampo, Kunimasa Saitoh
abstract: We propose and numerically demonstrate a method to achieve ultra-low birefringence in trench-assisted (TA) multi-core fibers (MCFs) by engineering the thermally induced stress field through the incorporation of doped silica stress rods. The approach is evaluated in two structures: a 4-core TA-MCF employing a single boron-doped rod at the cladding center, and an 8-core TA-MCF using one central and four peripheral fluorine-doped rods. By optimizing the rod positions, radii, and dopant concentrations, the method identifies parameter sets that reduce birefringence into the ultra-low regime by counteracting the stress asymmetries induced by neighboring cores. The optimized designs exhibit birefringence beat lengths exceeding 10 m and are expected to yield polarization-mode dispersion (PMD) on the order of 0.01 ps/√km with strong resilience to external perturbations. Moreover, the same stress balancing mechanism is expected to mitigate inter-core crosstalk (IC-XT) in certain MCF designs, thereby addressing the two critical impairments of PMD and IC-XT with a single approach.

title: Cloud Computing Resource Load Prediction Model Based on TCN-PatchTST
authors: Xiangyang Xue, Ziheng Xia, Yingxia Wang, Wenjun Huo
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: Addressing the dynamic, nonlinear, and multidimensional characteristics of cloud computing resource loads, this paper proposes TCN-PatchTST, a novel hybrid prediction model integrating Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) and the Patch Time-Series Transformer (PatchTST). The model leverages TCN's causal and dilated convolutions to extract salient local temporal features. Simultaneously, it employs PatchTST's patching mechanism and channel-independent modeling to capture complex long-term dependencies. A dedicated feature fusion layer is designed to effectively integrate these multiscale representations. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the Alibaba Cloud Cluster Trace v2018 dataset demonstrate that TCN-PatchTST significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baseline models, including Informer, Autoformer, and the original PatchTST. Our model achieves substantially reduced prediction errors (MSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.121$</tex>, MAE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.244)$</tex>, significantly enhancing CPU utilization prediction accuracy. These results provide robust technical support for intelligent cloud resource scheduling.

title: Enhancing Fault Tolerance in Microblaze V Soft-Core Processors: Performance and Reliability Assessment with ESA Benchmark Applications
authors: Gabriel Camargo, Fabio Benevenuti, José Rodrigo Azambuja, Antonio Carlos Beck, Fernanda Kastensmidt
abstract: RISC-V processors are increasingly considered for space applications due to their open-source nature, configurability, and suitability for implementing custom fault-tolerant designs. To better characterize their behavior in space-relevant scenarios, this paper evaluates both the performance and reliability of multiple MicroBlaze V soft-core configurations using the European Space Agency's OnBoard Processing Benchmark (OBPMark) applications. A selected subset of configurations was additionally hardened using the Xilinx Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) IP to assess the effectiveness of architectural redundancy in mitigating faults in SRAM-based FPGAs. All OBPMark applications were adapted to run in bare-metal mode, with results collected via serial telemetry. For reliability assessment, the workload of each application was adjusted so that each execution cycle lasted approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 ~ m s}$</tex>. Faults were then injected into the configuration memory through the ICAP interface, with no more than one fault injected per execution cycle. The combined performance and fault-injection results highlight the trade-offs between execution speed, FPGA resource usage, and robustness, offering guidance for designing RISC-V soft-core processors for space-grade FPGA platforms.

title: A Deep Learning Lora-Based Indoor Localization Technique
authors: Kamilia Aqqad, Karmel Aqqad, Naeema Khdeir, Raha Zabade, Wasel Ghanem, Ali Kadhim Lwas, Ashraf Al-Rimawi, Mohamed Hadi Habaebi, Alhareth Mohammed Zyoud
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Indoor localization is crucial for many applications where Global Positioning System (GPS) signals are frequently poor or unreliable, such as asset tracking and interior navigation. This study uses deep learning algorithms and the benefits of long-range wireless communication offered by Long Range (LoRa) to produce an accurate indoor locating system. The initial step towards constructing a wireless communication network is positioning LoRa-based devices at specified locations. A dataset is produced by merging the signal strength measurements acquired from each of these LoRa devices. Each location in the data set is within an environment, and measurements of the signal strength from LoRa devices related to those locations are included. The collected dataset is used to train the deep learning model. To increase accuracy, techniques like data augmentation and cross-validation were included. The experiment's results demonstrate the effectiveness of the deep learning-based LoRa indoor localization technique. Among the many possible applications for the suggested method are location-based services, asset monitoring, and precise indoor positioning.

title: Design and Simulation of Printed Force Sensitive Resistor for Wearable Electronics
authors: Muhammad Izzuddin Mohamad Azhar, Aliza Aini Md Ralib, Nor Hidayati Diyana Nordin, Anis Nurashikin Nordin, Rosminazuin Ab Rahim
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The growing demand of wearable electronics has motivated the development of printed force sensitive resistors that are flexible. Force sensitive resistors (FSRs) provide a promising solution as piezoresistive force sensors to sense force or pressure for wearable devices. The FSR shows a decrease in resistance when force is applied to the active surface area which has good potential application for flexible and wearable electronics. However, limited previous work studied on the finite element analysis to predict the change of resistance after sensing for the optimization before proceeding to the fabrication. Hence, this study aims to design and simulate a printed FSR for the optimization of design before proceeding to the fabrication of the printed sensor. Three important design parameters such as force applied, gap spacer height, and the electrode ratio were reported in this work. Finite element simulation results show the force applied onto the FSR can decrease its resistance until it reaches the minimum resistance value. As the gap spacer height increased, the minimum resistance value at a certain force also decreased. The electrode ratio (width of electrode/gap between electrodes) also influenced the maximum resistance drop of the FSR due to electrodes contacting the piezoresistive layer more evenly. The optimum parameters that were chosen in this work are the gap spacer height at 0.02 mm and electrode ratio (width of electrode/gap between electrodes) at 0.67. The simulation results in this work demonstrated that the height of gap spacer and the electrode ratio can influence the sensitivity of the FSR.

title: Social Media Analysis Using LSTM and Bi-LSTM for Automated Identification of Depression Cancer Patients
authors: Fasya Hanifah Putti, Muhammad Ary Murti, Astri Novianty, Casi Setianingsih, Nur Hafieza Ismail
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Cancer patients frequently experience mental health problems such as anxiety, stress, and depression as a result of the emotional strain of receiving a diagnosis and undergoing treatment. The inability of traditional detection techniques to reliably and effectively identify these circumstances emphasizes the need for more potent strategies. Social media offers a wealth of up-to-date information that can be used to monitor and comprehend patients mental health conditions. This study advances the development of an automated classification system that uses deep learning models to identify mental health issues in social media texts. We implemented two models: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM), using Word2Vec for text embedding. The models were initially trained and tested on a publicly available general dataset, split into 80 % for training and 20 % for testing. To evaluate real-world applicability, additional testing was conducted using scraped tweets related to cancer patients' mental health conditions. According to experimental results, the Bi-LSTM model outperformed the LSTM model, achieving 97% accuracy, 98% precision, 96% recall, and a 97 % F1 score. By contrast, the LSTM model obtained 96% accuracy, 98% precision, 96% recall, and 97% F1 score. These findings demonstrate how well Bi-LSTM can detect mental health problems in textual data, offering a valuable tool for cancer patients early detection and intervention plans.

title: Iterative Complex-Valued Quadratic-Cone Programming for Implementing Phase Equalizers
authors: Tian-Bo Deng
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Non-linear phase systems cannot retain the original signal waveforms after transmitting or processing digital signals. To remedy such phase distortions and retain the signal-waveform fidelity, phase equalizers are necessitated. This paper investigates how to implement an allpass phase equalizer (APE) via employing quadratic-cone programming (Qcone programming). The methodology first expresses the APE implementation as a Qcone programming with complex-valued Qcone constraints that comprise complex-valued vectors and matrices, and then performs the Qcone programming to generate an APE implementation solution. This strategy iteratively solves a series of Qcone programming problems, rendering its name iterative complex-valued Qcone programming. To exemplify its effectiveness, this paper showcases an APE implementation, which validates that this iterative complex-valued Qcone programming strategy can produce a remarkably accurate APE as compared to other existing iterative and non-iterative strategies.

title: Lightweight TinyML NIDS For IoT Devices
authors: Mohd Yusoff, Siti Sarah, Md Rafiqul Islam, Mohamed Hadi Habaebi, Athaurahman Najeeb
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: With the increase in the implementation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices, this has introduced some serious and critical security challenges, specifically with regards to the resource-constrained environments, which are IoT devices that are used by the public for convenience. Furthermore, traditional intrusion detection systems (IDS) are too computationally intensive for such devices, emphasizing on the need for a much lightweight and efficient alternatives. This paper focuses on developing a lightweight IDS using TinyML to tackle these security challenges and ensure safety in IoT devices. Public IoT datasets were preprocessed to enhance the quality of datasets, and it is followed by feature importance and class distribution analyses. The machine learning models, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Decision Tree, and Logistic Regression, were trained for intrusion detection. The models were then converted to C++ code format for deployment on a Raspberry Pi simulation. Testing and evaluation were conducted using opensource Quick Emulator (QEMU), which uses dynamic binary translation to emulate a computer's processor. QEMU provides a variety of hardware and device models for virtual machines, enabling it to run different guest operating systems, making it an ideal option. The testing and evaluation processes were conducted to assess performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and resource utilization. The results demonstrate that the proposed system achieves high detection accuracy with minimal computational overhead, making it a viable solution for securing resource-constrained IoT devices. This study underscores the potential of TinyML in improving and enhancing IoT security while ensuring scalability as well as efficiency.

title: Effects of Rain Rate With Various Integration Times on ITU-R P530-18 Attenuation Prediction
authors: Mohammad Rofiqul Hassan, Islam Md Rafiqul, Mohamed Hadi Habaebi, Asma Ali Budalal, M M Hasan Mahfuz, Khairayu Badron, Suriza A.Z.
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: All wireless communication systems are progressively transitioning to higher frequencies, which are significantly degraded by rainfall in outdoor environments. A dependable RF system can be designed using a globally accepted approach for accurately predicting rain attenuation, based on local rain intensity measurements. The necessary rain intensity for attenuation prediction is often measured at a single location with a 1 -minute integration period or converted from a longer integration time to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1}$</tex>-minute duration. Recent measurements of rain intensity with a 10 -second integration time indicate that intensity is not uniform across a 1 -minute duration, hence affecting the statistics of rain intensity distribution and attenuation predictions when measured with an integration time shorter than 1 minute. This paper presents the influence of integration times on rain rate statistics and eventually it's impact on rain attenuation prediction method. The distance factor proposed by ITU-R P530-18 is investigated by utilizing rain rate data collected with integration durations of 2 minutes, 1 minute, 30 seconds, 20 seconds, and 10 seconds. The effects are found not significant with any path length. Measured rain attenuation for a 300 m path at 26 GHz were compared to those predicted with rain rates measured in 5 integration times. For propagation paths longer than 1 km, the ITU-R model's 1minute integration time may be sufficient to “average out” the spatial inhomogeneity of the rain rate; however, for shorter propagation paths, the rain intensity with lower integration times reflect more accurate predictions.

title: Case Study: The High-Level Synthesis-Based Firmware Upgrade for the Digital Trigger Back-End System of the ATLAS Liquid Argon Calorimeter
authors: Melissa S. Aguiar, Dabson F. dos Santos, Adriana Milic, Diogo A. Cardinot, Marcos V. S. Oliveira, Luciano M. A. Filho, Gustavo B. Libotte, Bernardo S. Peralva
abstract: The trigger and data acquisition systems of the LHC experiments rely on reconfigurable architectures based on FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Arrays) to accommodate design updates and increasing of the detectors complexity. However, this flexibility comes at the cost of longer development and validation cycles, particularly for large real-time systems operating under strict latency and throughput constraints. This work presents the HLS-based firmware redesign of the back-end system from the ATLAS Liquid Argon Calorimeter, where the introduction of a parallel switch matrix on REMAP block significantly simplifies the architecture and reduces the use of firmware resources. The proposed methodology allows systematic design exploration without modifying the source code, shortens verification time, and provides a more efficient workflow for creating and maintaining large-scale FPGA firmware in modern trigger systems.

title: Case Study: A Uniform Benchmarking of RISC-V Cores on FPGA — Performance, Power, and Area Analysis
authors: João Carlos Britto Filho, Paulo Francisco Butzen
abstract: Selecting an open-source RISC-V core for FPGA deployment requires balancing performance, power, and area—yet most published comparisons evaluate only one dimension or rely on vendor estimation tools. This paper presents a uniform benchmarking of 12 open-source RISC-V cores on a Cyclone IV FPGA, combining the CoreMark benchmark with physical power measurements from an INA219 sensor sampled at 20 kHz. Under identical synthesis and operating conditions, results reveal up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6 \times$</tex> variation in area (869-5,374 logic elements), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.6 \times$</tex> in performance (75-272.5 CoreMarks), and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times$</tex> in power (57-171 mW). We further derive efficiency metrics (CoreMarks/mW and CoreMarks/LE) that expose non-obvious trade-offs, such as the PULP RI5CY core achieving the highest power efficiency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.26 \text{C M} / \text{m W}$</tex>) despite moderate area, while the RISCV-HoneyBee core delivers the best area efficiency (0.104 CM/LE) with the smallest footprint.

title: Design and Implementation of a Lightweight Simulation Testing Tool for Automatic Train Supervision (ATS) Systems in Intelligent Transportation Systems
authors: Huanhuan Hu, Xiaping Huang, Na Lin
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: With the advancement of automation in urban rail transit, the Automatic Train Supervision (ATS) system—serving as the core dispatch and control platform for subway operations—plays a critical role in ensuring train operational efficiency and safety. However, traditional ATS testing relies on simulation platforms for external subsystems such as Zone Controllers (ZC), Carborne Controllers (CC), and Computer-Based Interlocking (CBI), leading to complex test environments, high resource consumption, and inefficiencies due to multi-system coupling issues. To address these challenges, this paper proposes ATSSIM (Simulation for ATS), a simulation testing tool designed to emulate communication interfaces with external subsystems. By mimicking subsystem behaviors through bidirectional message interaction, ATSSIM creates a lightweight and flexible standalone testing environment, eliminating dependencies on redundant subsystems. This enables testers to focus on validating ATS interface functionalities and logical operations without interference from multi-system synchronization. The paper details the architectural design, communication protocols, and dynamic simulation capabilities of ATSSIM. Practical case studies demonstrate that the tool significantly reduces test environment setup costs while improving execution efficiency. The research outcomes provide an efficient and reliable technical solution for functional verification and testing efficiency optimization of ATS systems, offering valuable insights for enhancing testing methodologies in rail transit signal systems.

title: Scatter Search, Ejection Chains, and Filter-and-Fan for Vehicle Routing: A Unified Framework
authors: Cesar Rego
abstract: We develop a scatter search algorithm for the classical Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) with capacity and route length constraints. The VRP involves determining the optimal set of routes from a central hub to set of demand points in a network, with the goal of minimizing the total travel distance while ensuring no route exceeds the capacity of the servicing resource or the allowed total travel and service time. This problem naturally arises in logistics, transportation, distribution, and communication networks. The VRP has been extensively studied, leading to the development of numerous solution methods and algorithms. Given its classification as an NP-Hard problem, heuristic algorithms are often preferred for finding high-quality solutions within practical time limits. Our approach combines the efficiency and intensification capabilities of the filter-and-fan and ejection chain methods with the diversification and adaptive memory programming strategies embodied in scatter search methodology. Emphasis is placed on developing algorithmic designs that facilitate replication, fine-tuning, and extension to other settings, while remaining highly effective and efficient.

title: TacMan-Turbo: Proactive Tactile Control for Robust and Efficient Articulated Object Manipulation_supp4-3683863.mp4
authors: Zihang Zhao
abstract: Adept manipulation of articulated objects is essential for robots to operate successfully in human environments. Such manipulation requires both effectiveness—reliable operation despite uncertain object structures—and efficiency—swift execution with minimal redundant steps and smooth trajectories. Existing approaches struggle to achieve both objectives simultaneously: methods relying on predefined kinematic models lack robustness when encountering structural variations, while the tactile-informed approach achieves robust manipulation but sacrifices efficiency through reactive, step-by-step execute-and-recover cycles. To address this challenge, this paper introduces TacMan-Turbo, a proactive tactile control framework that unifies the cycles into a continuous control loop. Our key insight is to interpret tactile signals temporally in addition to spatially: instead of treating contact deviations merely as instantaneous error signals requiring immediate compensation, we analyze sequential tactile observations to reveal local kinematic information. This temporal perspective enables our controller to predict future object states and proactively modulate manipulation velocities, eliminating the previous execute-and-recover cycle. In evaluations across 200 diverse simulated articulated objects and real-world experiments, our approach maintains a near-perfect performance while significantly enhancing time efficiency, action efficiency, and trajectory smoothness (all p-values < 0.0001). These results demonstrate that tactile feedback serves a dual purpose: providing not only spatial error signals for reactive control but also critical temporal structural information that enables proactive prediction and control. By enabling robots to manipulate articulated objects both reliably and efficiently, this work advances robot capabilities for seamless operation in dynamic, human-centric environments.

title: AI-Tuned Smart Potentiometric based Sensor for Chloride Concentration Assessment in Sweat
authors: D. De Venuto, D. Loconte, M. Chiarantoni, D. Caputo, G. De Cesare, N. Lovecchio, G. Petrucci, F. Cappelli, M. Ruta, E. Di Sciascio
abstract: This paper presents a wearable potentiometric sensor system for sweat chloride measurement, targeting cystic fibrosis screening. The platform integrates Ag/AgCl electrodes, an instrumentation amplifier, and analog signal conditioning to enable low-noise acquisition of electrochemical signals. A low-power microcontroller performs on-device signal preprocessing and implements a TinyML-based regression model for sensor calibration and chloride concentration estimation. Environmental validation is achieved through an integrated humidity sensor to ensure reliable sweat detection. Experimental results demonstrate accurate chloride quantification within the clinically relevant diagnostic range, while maintaining low power consumption and autonomous operation, making the proposed system suitable for point-of-care and wearable sensing applications. This study validates the feasibility of deploying "smart," self-contained sensors that can autonomously map raw potentiometric transients to clinical values.

title: Rotation-symmetric bent functions outside the completed Maiorana-McFarland class
authors: Alexandr Polujan, Sadmir Kudin, Enes Pasalic
abstract: Rotation-symmetric (RS) bent functions, which are invariant under the action of the cyclic group, have attracted significant attention over the past three decades due to their importance in cryptographic applications. For the last three decades of research on these objects, most known RS bent functions have been obtained by applying extended-affine equivalence to specific Maiorana-McFarland bent functions, in a way that ensures the resulting function retains invariance under the cyclic group action. Due to the intrinsic difficulty of characterizing RS bent functions that do not belong to the completed Maiorana-McFarland class M#, there has been no evidence for the existence of such functions until now. In this paper, we provide, for the first time, a solution to this problem. First, we perform a computational classification of RS cubic bent functions in ten variables under extended-affine equivalence and demonstrate that one of the resulting classes is outside the M# class. Next, we prove that an infinite family of RS bent functions on Fn2 of maximum algebraic degree n/2 (Su, Adv. Math. Commun. 13(2): 253–265, 2019) does not belong to M#, for all n ≥ 8. Finally, we show that a family of quartic RS bent functions on Fn2 (Carlet, Gao, and Liu, J. Comb. Theory Ser. A 127: 161–175, 2014), does not belong to the M# class for infinitely many n.

title: MFEFNet: An Efficient Network for Infrared Small Target Detection with Multi-View Feature Extraction and Multi-Scale Attention Fusion
authors: Liu Zhang, Zongchen Zhao, Xueying Lv, Xuanyun Zhang, Wenhua Wang, Jiabao Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains a core challenge in remote sensing, mainly due to low signal-to-noise ratios, small target sizes, background interference, and large scale variations. Although existing methods have achieved progress, they still suffer from limited representation ability of conventional convolutions, weak feature enhancement, and insufficient multi-scale feature fusion, which restrict detection performance and practical application. To alleviate these issues, this paper introduces a dedicated multi-aspect feature extraction and fusion network (MFEFNet). The proposed framework systematically addresses the above challenges via three innovative components: the multi-shape receptive field extraction module (MSRFEM), the spatial-channel-global-local joint re-extraction module (SCGLJRM), and the multi-scale cross-attention mechanism (MSCAM). Specifically, MSRFEM leverages dilated deformable convolutions and the Res2 architecture to adaptively capture multi-scale and multi-shape target characteristics as well as locally correlated features. SCGLJRM enhances global context modeling across spatial and channel dimensions by aggregating complementary local-global information through a cascade of local spatial-local channel (LL), local spatial-global channel (GC), and global spatial-local channel (LC) submodules. MSCAM mitigates the feature fusion imbalance in typical Transformer-based cross-attention by performing correlation matrix transposition and fusion before integrating with feature maps, thereby strengthening the mutual enhancement of unique and shared information across different scales. Notably, MFEFNet breaks the constraints of fixed input image resolution, supporting flexible training and inference with arbitrary image sizes. Extensive experiments conducted on SIRST, IRSTD-1K, and NUDT-SIRST datasets consistently verify that MFEFNet achieves superior detection accuracy over numerous state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods and maintains strong generalization ability under complex backgrounds, demonstrating its effectiveness and superiority.

title: Kinetic Energy Based Power Smoothing Method for High-Inertia Wind Turbines Using ESO-MFPCC
authors: Ying Zhu, Xinhang Zhou, Zhinong Wei
abstract: Large capacity wind turbines have the characteristic of high inertia, which further provides conditions for leveraging the stored kinetic energy to regulate and smooth the power output. However, the high inertia will make it difficult to tune the proportional-integral (PI) parameters of the machine side control link and challenge the transient behavior and responsiveness of the control strategy. To stabilize output power and achieve dynamic performance improvement, this article proposes a kinetic energy based power smoothing method for high-inertia wind turbines using model-free predictive current control based on extended state observer (ESO-MFPCC). By cascading a second order filter (SOF) in the active power control loop, the output power fluctuation of the wind turbine is reduced effectively. The ESO-MFPCC scheme eliminates the dependence on system parameters, thereby solving the PI controller tuning problem and enhancing the dynamic responsiveness of high-inertia wind turbines. Finally, both simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively smooths the output power: in the 5 min stochastic complex wind-speed simulation, the mean power variation rate is reduced by approximately 50%. Moreover, in the 15 s step-wind simulation, the phase-A current total harmonic distortion is 1.35%, indicating a low harmonic distortion level.

title: A Dual-Event-Driven Fusion Estimation Method for Road Friction Coefficient Based on Image and Vehicle Dynamics
authors: Weihua Wang, Shuo Bai, Haonan Ding, Dawei Pi, Guodong Yin
abstract: The road friction coefficient reflects the tire's ability to grip the road surface and directly affects a vehicle's acceleration, braking, and steering performance. Therefore, real-time and accurate detection of the road friction coefficient plays an irreplaceable role in ensuring safe vehicle operation and enabling intelligent traffic management. To address limitations such as the excitation constraints of single dynamic estimators, the low accuracy and high computational demand of standalone image recognition, this paper proposes a dual-event-driven fusion estimation framework that integrates visual perception and dynamic information. Deep fusion is performed at the data, model, and decision levels. In the image recognition module, kinematic constraints are applied to image data, and a Multi-Patch Fusion Decision Network (MFDNet) is designed. Additionally, a dual-event-driven (DED) mechanism is introduced by defining a visual confidence factor and a state evaluation metric, which jointly trigger decision-level fusion.During model fusion, a Dual-Event-Driven Adaptive Cubature Kalman Filter (DED-ACKF) is developed. It dynamically adjusts the filter gain based on residuals between image-based and dynamics-based estimation results using a time-varying scaling factor. Simulation and road test results demonstrate that the proposed estimation system achieves both predictability and accuracy, demonstrating robustness across all driving conditions.

title: Improved Prediction of Secondary Flows in a Square Duct for Blanket First Wall Using a Nonlinear Eddy-Viscosity Model
authors: Ying Xu
abstract: Parallel square ducts or rectangular channels are a widely adopted design concept in the blanket first wall (FW) cooling. Flow in the cooling channel is often fully turbulent (Reynold number greater than ${10}^{4}$ ) with secondary motions in the cross-stream plane. Popular k- $\varepsilon $ and k- $\omega $ turbulence models are widely used in computational fluid dynamics (CFDs) simulation of engineering applications due to their low cost and ease of use. However, these models often assume a linear Reynolds stress model, i.e., the Reynolds stress is linearly proportional to the local mean rate-of-strain. As a result, no secondary motions can be observed with the linear assumption. Therefore, a turbulence model with the nonlinear Reynolds stress assumption is necessary for duct and channel flows. In this article, the generalized k- $\omega $ (GEKO) model developed by Ansys is tuned to include an additional term in the Reynolds stress tensor to account for the secondary motions. It can be shown that the nonlinear eddy-viscosity model (NLEVM) significantly improves the prediction of secondary flows and anisotropic turbulence compared to the linear eddy-viscosity model (LEVM). The simulation results are validated against the direct numerical simulation (DNS) data, demonstrating that NLEVM provides a more accurate representation of velocity profiles, Reynolds stresses, and local wall shear stress distributions. This study highlights the importance of using appropriate turbulence models for accurate CFD simulations in engineering applications involving complex flow phenomena.

title: DECOR+: Energy-Balanced Representation Enhancement for Long-Tailed Remote Sensing Scene Recognition
authors: Jianlin Xie, Guanqun Wang, Tong Zhang, Yin Zhuang, He Chen, Liang Chen, Jun Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing (RS) imagery, characterized by wide geographic coverage and highly diverse land-cover categories, naturally exhibits severe long-tailed distributions. Such imbalance causes head classes to dominate both feature learning and gradient optimization, leading to degraded separability, overlapping feature distributions, and biased decision boundaries. We interpret these effects through a unified energy imbalance perspective, where head classes accumulate disproportionate spectral, geometric, and statistical representation energy, while tail classes remain weakly expressed. To address this issue, we propose DECOR+ (Energy-Balanced Representation Enhancement), a unified framework that restores multi-domain energy equilibrium for long-tailed RS scene recognition. DECOR+ consists of three complementary components. First, a spectral balancing module that employs a learnable frequency-domain filter to selectively enhance high-frequency structural cues typically suppressed in tail classes. Second, a geometric balancing module based on polar dual-prototype supervised contrastive learning, which enforces angular alignment and radial compactness to achieve a uniformly organized embedding space. Third, a statistical balancing module that performs Gaussian tail modeling to synthesize variance-regularized features, enriching tail-class diversity and equalizing gradient contributions during optimization. Comprehensive experiments on multiple long-tailed benchmarks, including NWPU-RESISC45-LT (NWPU-LT), AID-LT, and SIRI-WHU-LT, demonstrate that DECOR+ consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods, particularly under severe imbalance. These results validate the effectiveness of energy-balanced representation learning and highlight the potential of DECOR+ as a principled solution for robust and equitable RS image interpretation. Source code is available at https://github.com/ChloeeGrace/DECOR PLUS.

title: Lateral Dynamics Modeling and Analysis in Electric Vehicles according to the Battery Pack Arrangement
authors: Rodrigo Gutierrez-Garza, Jorge De J. Lozoya-Santos, Juan C. Tudon-Martinez, Riccardo Cespi, Stefano Di Gennaro
abstract: This study provides a multibody dynamics analysis to evaluate how different Battery Pack (BP) configurations impact vehicle dynamics, particularly for a GEM e6 Electric Vehicle (EV). One configuration introduces a new BP with Li-Ion cells, while a non-sealed Flood Lead-Acid (FLA) BP is considered as the Baseline configuration case; this latter BP represents the commercially available BP for this vehicle. Based on the classic bicycle model, the paper proposes an extension of this model by incorporating varying parameters that modify the lateral vehicle dynamics according to the BP configuration. The study uses a mathematical model tuned with experimental data and a multibody dynamics model using Matlab/Simscape. Analysis shows that the lateral dynamics of the GEM e6 is significantly affected by the weight and distribution of the BP, with a lateral velocity effect of 0.2112 m/s with the Design 1 that features the original FLA BP reorganized and an average of 7.470 m/s for the designs equipped with a modified Li-Ion BP for a Double Lane Change (DLC) maneuver. From a modeling perspective, the proposed extension of the bicycle model is well-adjusted to the different BP configurations. The results demonstrate the importance of conducting this type of analysis during the design of a BP, focusing on its impact on driving performance and maneuverability.

title: PrivTI: Efficient End-to-End Privacy-Preserving Inference for Transformer-based Models in MLaaS
authors: Mingshun Luo, Haolei He, Wenti Yang, Meng Li, Longfei Wu, Zijian Zhang, Zhitao Guan
abstract: Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) offers convenient access to high-quality transformer-based model inference without local deployment, but it requires uploading sensitive data to the server, raising significant privacy concerns. Recent advances in homomorphic encryption (HE) and secure multi-party computation (MPC) have shown great potential for enabling privacy-preserving inference. However, existing schemes face challenges with transformer-based models due to massive matrix multiplications and complex non-linear functions. To address these challenges, we propose PrivTI, an efficient end-to-end privacy-preserving inference framework for transformer-based models. Specifically, we design an HE-based single-instruction multiple-data (SIMD) encoding scheme to perform matrix multiplication efficiently. To handle non-linear functions, we develop MPC-friendly protocols for core functions like GELU, Softmax, and LayerNorm, and further extend support to SiLU and RMSNorm. Moreover, PrivTI supports conversions between HE and MPC, enabling end-to-end privacy-preserving inference. Experimental results demonstrate that PrivTI achieves speedups 1.9-45.2× in matrix multiplication and 1.1-18.9× in non-linear function evaluation compared to baseline schemes IRON, BOLT, and NEXUS under the Local Area Network (LAN) setting. Additionally, PrivTI reduces communication overhead by 1.1-4898.7×. Moreover, end-to-end benchmarks show 3.3-9.8× improvements in computation speed and 1.6-6.2× reductions in communication overhead.

title: Decentralized Real-Time Train-to-Grid Control by Smoothly Homotopic Trajectory Transition
authors: Zihan He, Can Wan, Hui Liu, Jinshuo Su, Haimin Xie
abstract: Electrified rail transportation possesses significant potential to support the real-time power balance of the power systems under high penetration of renewable energy, due to the high power capacity and fast response capability. However, direct power control is fundamentally incompatible with modern electric trains’ operation, as their running processes are governed by automatic train control systems via tracking predefined reference trajectories. This paper proposes a universal real-time train-to-grid control method that is compatible with the train operation as well as the designated power response command. To reflect the power response command in train trajectory, the sliding dynamics of the reference time is backward deduced based on the kinetic model of the train. To restore operations and ensure punctuality, the generated unconstrained power response trajectory is gradually deformed into the scheduled trajectory by smoothly homotopic transition. Then the transition trajectory is further rectified to ensure the compliance with the track speed limit. Benefiting from the compatibility of trajectory synthesis and power response design, simultaneous inertia and droop type frequency response from electric trains is realized for the first time. Comprehensive simulations based on various trains and railways validate the effectiveness of the proposed method and demonstrate the considerable real-time grid supporting capability of electric trains.

title: Rep-MMB: Bridging Mobile CNN and Transformer for Sensor-Based Human Activity Recognition
authors: Jinsheng Liu, Lei Zhang, Xin Liu, Guangjie Chen, Zenan Fu, Wenbo Huang, Hao Wu, Aiguo Song
abstract: Lightweight CNNs and Transformers have shown great promise in sensor-based human activity recognition (HAR), yet their structural synergies remain underexplored. This paper bridges this gap by integrating the MetaFormer paradigm—a general architecture abstracted from Transformers that structurally separates token mixing (i.e., self-attention) and channel mixing (i.e., feed-forward networks)—into efficient CNN design. While MetaFormer offers a powerful inductive bias, its standard self-attention mechanism is often computationally intensive for resource-constrained HAR. To address this, we revolutionize the classic MobileNetV3 architecture from a MetaFormer perspective, introducing Rep-MMB, a new family of pure lightweight CNNs. By leveraging structural reparameterization, Rep-MMB decouples multi-branch training-time complexity from efficient single-branch inference, enabling high accuracy with low latency. Evaluations on four public HAR benchmarks show that Rep-MMB outperforms state-of-the-art lightweight models in accuracy and efficiency, with practical validation on embedded devices. We hope that Rep-MMB may serve as a strong baseline to inspire future edge-deployed HAR research.

title: An Electrolytic-Capacitorless Integrated LDC/V2L Converter for Hybrid Electric Vehicles
authors: Gyeong-Hyun Kwon, Dong-In Lee, Hong-Seong Kim, Jun-Sik Hwang, Yeonho Jeong, Han-Shin Youn
abstract: This paper presents an integrated power converter architecture targeting vehicle-to-load (V2L) functionality in hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs). In the proposed configuration, the primary-side switches of the low-voltage dc/dc converter (LDC) are integrated with the power switches and magnetic components of the boost converter used in the V2L stage. This structural integration also enables the elimination of the dc-link capacitor, typically required at the input of the inverter stage, resulting in significant reductions in component count and circuit complexity. The reliable operation of the proposed converter is experimentally validated under three operating modes, including driving, stationary V2L, and driving with V2L. The prototype hardware is designed for a total power of 3 kW, with 2 kW allocated to the LDC and 1 kW to the V2L function. The converter achieves a peak efficiency of 94.46% in the driving mode, and a peak efficiency of 96.87% in both stationary V2L and driving with V2L modes. Moreover, the electrolytic-capacitorless implementation and reduced dedicated V2L boost elements enable a compact, cost-effective, and less complex implementation.

title: Forward-Secure Identity-Based Signcryption with Equality Test for Social Recommendation on Smart IoT Devices
authors: Wenjie Yang, Yu Cai, Qunhao Ou, Futai Zhang, Anjia Yang
abstract: With the deep integration of smart IoT devices with social networks, platforms use user-interest similarity for social recommendations to connect like-minded people. Identity-based encryption with equality test (IBEET) enables equivalence testing of interest ciphertexts encrypted under different public keys and avoids costly certificate management. It is beneficial for privacy-preserving social recommendations on smart IoT devices. Nevertheless, existing IBEET schemes suffer from two critical flaws: the lack of forward security allows historical trapdoor holders to test newly generated ciphertexts, and the absence of ciphertext origin authentication impedes the legality verification of the ciphertext source. In this paper, taking into account IoT-device features like limited resources, we introduce a forward secure identity-based signcryption scheme with equality test enabling public verification (FS-IBSCET-PV) for social recommendation. It enables the platforms to leverage users’ trapdoors to perform equality tests on interest ciphertexts, accurately matching users who share common interests. Meanwhile, users can submit time-limited authorized trapdoors when needing recommendation services, thus effectively reducing the risk of trapdoor misuse. Furthermore, upon receiving recommendation results, users can publicly verify their origin without extra decryption to ensure the authenticity of the recommendations, consequently mitigating the hardware overhead. Security analysis and simulation experiments show that FS-IBSCET-PV has enhanced functionality and better testing efficiency than related schemes. It provides a secure and practical solution for privacy-preserving social recommendations on resource-limited smart IoT devices.

title: Covert Communication in Federated Learning
authors: Sang Wu Kim, Chenyu Xu
abstract: This letter presents a covert communication frame-work for federated learning (FL) systems, in which the central server embeds hidden messages into global model updates that naturally exhibit a moving-target property, thereby complicating adversarial detection and tracking. The proposed embedding strategy preserves the statistical characteristics of standard updates, ensuring covertness via statistical indistinguishability. We analytically characterize the decoding error probability and the achievable covert transmission rate under Rayleigh fading channels, and evaluate the resulting expressions through experiments on standard FL benchmarks. The results demonstrate that covert message embedding introduces negligible impact on learning performance, with model accuracy converging to baseline levels after sufficient training rounds.

title: Vegetation Health Prediction Using RGB Images from UAV with Deep Learning
authors: Masud Rana, Ram Ray, Xishuang Dong, Lijun Qian
abstract: Vegetation health prediction is one of the most important research questions in precision agriculture. The Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) is one of the most widely adopted indicators of crop vigor and stress. Traditional NDVI estimation relies on multispectral or hyperspectral imaging, which is expensive and inaccessible for small-scale farming. Recent advances in deep learning enable the prediction of NDVI from low-cost RGB imagery; however, the limited availability of paired RGB-NDVI datasets and domain discrepancies between existing sources hinder robust generalization. In this work, we construct two UAV-based RGB-NDVI paired datasets and investigate multiple deep learning architectures, including an FCNN, an RSCNN, and an ADL, for NDVI prediction. Our experimental results show that RSCNN consistently delivers the strongest regression performance across both datasets, achieving R2 values of 82.43% and 89.53%, and the lowest MAE of 1.87% and 1.57% on two of the created datasets, respectively. Furthermore, we studied transfer learning and proposed a self-learning approach that relied on pseudo-labeled target samples. The results demonstrate improved performance that varied with the chosen confidence threshold and the transfer direction.

title: A Novel Design of Pulsed Magnet for Repetitive Magnetic Field
authors: Yihang Huang, Huidong Shang, Shaobo Liu, Aoming Ge, Shuang Wang, Liang Li, Yiliang Lv, Tao Peng
abstract: Repetitive pulsed magnetic fields play a critical role in neutron diffraction, room-temperature magnetic refrigeration, and pulsed terahertz gyrotron. In this article, a novel design combining flux concentrator (FC) and coil with cooling channels is proposed. This technique mitigates the thermal load generated in the coil by transferring part of it to the FC, thus enhancing both the magnetic field strength and the repetition rate. Comprehensive simulations of the electromagnetic, thermal, and mechanical behaviors were conducted, showing that the maximum temperature is 430 K and the maximum stress is 57.7MPa. A 10-T pulsed magnetic field at a repetition rate of 5 Hz was successfully generated.

title: Supraharmonic Measurement Based on Windowed Compressive Sensing and Orthogonal Matching Pursuit
authors: Chengbin Liang, Yuanda Liu, Guoping Zhang, Tenghua Yin, Zhaosheng Teng, Jianmin Li, Shiyan Hu
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy sources and widespread application of high-frequency power electronic devices, the issue of supraharmonics in power systems has become increasingly prominent, which imposes a significant challenge to power quality. To mitigate the impact of spectral leakage and the picket-fence effect on the accuracy of supraharmonic disturbance parameter measurements, and reduce the sampling pressure of supraharmonic signal, this article proposes a supraharmonic measurement method based on windowed compressed sensing (CS) and the orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP) algorithm. The four-term third-order Nuttall window function is integrated into CS technology, where a windowed sparse measurement matrix is constructed to enable windowed compressed sampling of supraharmonic signals. Subsequently, the OMP algorithm is used for frequency estimation of the supraharmonic signals, and the three-spectral-line interpolation technique is applied to reduce the picket-fence effect, improving the accuracy of supraharmonic disturbance frequency, amplitude, and phase measurements. Simulation experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithm significantly addresses the spectral leakage and the picket-fence effect, effectively reduces the data required for supraharmonic detection, and improves the measurement accuracy while improving noise resistance. In practical experiments, the absolute errors in frequency and amplitude measurement for three sets of supraharmonic components are 0.55, 0.36, and 1.09 Hz and 0.0006, 0.0008, and 0.001 V, respectively, validating the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed method.
